title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore the history and significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. They will learn about its traditional role as a food source and transportation route for Ngāti Kahungunu, the impact of colonial land use changes, and contemporary restoration efforts led by local hapū.
learningObjectives: Analyze historical and contemporary relationships between Ngāti Kahungunu and Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku through primary sources and oral histories
Evaluate the social, economic, and environmental impacts of land use changes on the awa and surrounding community since colonial settlement
Design and propose a culturally-informed kaitiakitanga action plan addressing a current challenge facing the awa
Articulate personal connections to place and responsibilities as kaitiaki through reflective writing and discussion

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa: Connecting to Our Waterway
activities.description: Students begin with a karakia and waiata for the awa. They examine historical maps, photos, and Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau (stories) about Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, creating an annotated timeline that highlights key events in its history and relationship with people.

activities.title: Hīkoi and Observation: Seeing the Awa Today
activities.description: Guided by a kaumātua from local hapū, students take a hīkoi (walk) along a section of the stream. They practice mindful observation, sketching the environment, noting water quality indicators, and identifying both natural features and human impacts. They learn traditional names for native plants and fish species found there.

activities.title: Voices of the Awa: Community Perspectives
activities.description: In small groups, students prepare and conduct short interviews (in person or via video call) with diverse community stakeholders: a local farmer, a representative from the Regional Council's environmental team, a member of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream Care Group, and a kaumātua. They develop questions focused on perceptions of the stream's health, value, and future.

activities.title: Te Pae Tawhiti: Visioning for the Future
activities.description: Using the knowledge gathered, groups synthesize the different perspectives and historical data to identify a primary challenge facing the awa (e.g., pollution, invasive species, water flow). They brainstorm potential solutions, evaluating them against criteria of cultural appropriateness, environmental sustainability, and social feasibility.

activities.title: Presenting the Kaitiakitanga Plan
activities.description: Groups formalize their proposed solution into a concise Kaitiakitanga Action Plan. They create a digital presentation or a physical poster that outlines the issue, their proposed action (e.g., a community planting day, a design for a raingarden, an education campaign), and how it upholds the principles of kaitiakitanga. They present these to the class and invited community experts for feedback.

resources: Kaumātua from local Ngāti Kahungunu hapū (e.g., Aorangi or Ngāi Te Whatuiāpiti) for guiding hīkoi and sharing mātauranga
Hawke's Bay Regional Council 'Land Use Map' and 'State of the Environment' report webpages for the Mangakōtukutuku catchment
The book 'Ngāti Kahungunu: The History' by J. H. Mitchell for historical context and pūrākau
Digital voice recorders or tablets for conducting and recording stakeholder interviews

tasks: Annotated historical timeline of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Synthesis report of community interview findings
Group Kaitiakitanga Action Plan proposal (digital or poster)
Individual written reflection on personal role as a kaitiaki

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L5: Understand how the ideas and actions of people in the past have had a significant impact on people's lives
Social Studies L5: Understand how people's management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability
Social Studies L5: Understand how cultural interaction impacts on cultures and societies
Social Studies L5: Understand that people have social, cultural, and economic roles, rights, and responsibilities
